View C++ Code (8228 chars)
#include <algorithm>
#include <cstdint>
#include <iostream>
#include <limits>
#include <numeric>
#include <queue>
#include <tuple>
#include <vector>

using namespace std;

struct Edge {
    int u;
    int v;
    long long w;
};

struct AdjEdge {
    int to;
    long long w;
    int id;
};

struct DSU {
    vector<int> p;
    vector<int> r;

    explicit DSU(int n = 0) {
        init(n);
    }

    void init(int n) {
        p.resize(n);
        r.assign(n, 0);
        iota(p.begin(), p.end(), 0);
    }

    int find(int x) {
        while (p[x] != x) {
            p[x] = p[p[x]];
            x = p[x];
        }
        return x;
    }

    bool unite(int a, int b) {
        a = find(a);
        b = find(b);
        if (a == b) return false;
        if (r[a] < r[b]) swap(a, b);
        p[b] = a;
        if (r[a] == r[b]) ++r[a];
        return true;
    }
};

struct Candidate {
    int a;
    int b;
    long long cost;
    int u;
    int v;
    int bridgeEdge;
};

static const long long INF = numeric_limits<long long>::max() / 4;

int main() {
    ios::sync_with_stdio(false);
    cin.tie(nullptr);

    int n, m, kInput;
    if (!(cin >> n >> m >> kInput)) {
        return 0;
    }

    vector<Edge> edges;
    edges.reserve(m);
    vector<vector<AdjEdge>> g(n);

    for (int i = 0; i < m; ++i) {
        int u, v;
        long long w;
        cin >> u >> v >> w;
        edges.push_back({u, v, w});
        g[u].push_back({v, w, i});
        g[v].push_back({u, w, i});
    }

    vector<int> terminals(kInput);
    for (int i = 0; i < kInput; ++i) {
        cin >> terminals[i];
    }

    sort(terminals.begin(), terminals.end());
    terminals.erase(unique(terminals.begin(), terminals.end()), terminals.end());
    const int k = static_cast<int>(terminals.size());

    if (k <= 1) {
        cout << 0 << ' ' << 0 << '\n';
        return 0;
    }

    vector<char> isTerminal(n, false);
    for (int t : terminals) isTerminal[t] = true;

    vector<long long> dist(n, INF);
    vector<int> owner(n, -1);
    vector<int> parent(n, -1);
    vector<int> parentEdge(n, -1);

    using State = tuple<long long, int, int>; // dist, owner, vertex
    priority_queue<State, vector<State>, greater<State>> pq;

    for (int i = 0; i < k; ++i) {
        int s = terminals[i];
        if (0 < dist[s] || (dist[s] == 0 && (owner[s] == -1 || i < owner[s]))) {
            dist[s] = 0;
            owner[s] = i;
            parent[s] = -1;
            parentEdge[s] = -1;
            pq.push({0, i, s});
        }
    }

    while (!pq.empty()) {
        auto [d, own, v] = pq.top();
        pq.pop();

        if (d != dist[v] || own != owner[v]) continue;

        for (const auto& e : g[v]) {
            if (d > INF - e.w) continue;
            long long nd = d + e.w;
            int to = e.to;
            if (nd < dist[to] || (nd == dist[to] && (owner[to] == -1 || own < owner[to]))) {
                dist[to] = nd;
                owner[to] = own;
                parent[to] = v;
                parentEdge[to] = e.id;
                pq.push({nd, own, to});
            }
        }
    }

    vector<Candidate> candidates;
    candidates.reserve(m);

    for (int id = 0; id < m; ++id) {
        int u = edges[id].u;
        int v = edges[id].v;
        int a = owner[u];
        int b = owner[v];
        if (a < 0 || b < 0 || a == b) continue;
        if (dist[u] >= INF || dist[v] >= INF) continue;

        int uu = u;
        int vv = v;
        if (a > b) {
            swap(a, b);
            swap(uu, vv);
        }

        long long cost = dist[uu] + edges[id].w + dist[vv];
        candidates.push_back({a, b, cost, uu, vv, id});
    }

    sort(candidates.begin(), candidates.end(), [](const Candidate& x, const Candidate& y) {
        if (x.a != y.a) return x.a < y.a;
        if (x.b != y.b) return x.b < y.b;
        if (x.cost != y.cost) return x.cost < y.cost;
        return x.bridgeEdge < y.bridgeEdge;
    });

    vector<Candidate> pairEdges;
    pairEdges.reserve(candidates.size());
    for (size_t i = 0; i < candidates.size();) {
        size_t j = i;
        Candidate best = candidates[i];
        while (j < candidates.size() && candidates[j].a == candidates[i].a && candidates[j].b == candidates[i].b) {
            if (candidates[j].cost < best.cost ||
                (candidates[j].cost == best.cost && candidates[j].bridgeEdge < best.bridgeEdge)) {
                best = candidates[j];
            }
            ++j;
        }
        pairEdges.push_back(best);
        i = j;
    }

    sort(pairEdges.begin(), pairEdges.end(), [](const Candidate& x, const Candidate& y) {
        if (x.cost != y.cost) return x.cost < y.cost;
        if (x.a != y.a) return x.a < y.a;
        if (x.b != y.b) return x.b < y.b;
        return x.bridgeEdge < y.bridgeEdge;
    });

    DSU dsuTerm(k);
    vector<Candidate> terminalMST;
    terminalMST.reserve(k - 1);

    for (const auto& c : pairEdges) {
        if (dsuTerm.unite(c.a, c.b)) {
            terminalMST.push_back(c);
            if (static_cast<int>(terminalMST.size()) == k - 1) break;
        }
    }

    if (static_cast<int>(terminalMST.size()) != k - 1) {
        cout << -1 << ' ' << 0 << '\n';
        return 0;
    }

    vector<char> inSubgraph(m, false);

    auto addPathToOwner = [&](int x) {
        while (x >= 0 && parent[x] != -1) {
            int eid = parentEdge[x];
            if (eid >= 0) inSubgraph[eid] = true;
            x = parent[x];
        }
    };

    for (const auto& c : terminalMST) {
        addPathToOwner(c.u);
        inSubgraph[c.bridgeEdge] = true;
        addPathToOwner(c.v);
    }

    vector<int> subEdges;
    subEdges.reserve(m);
    for (int id = 0; id < m; ++id) {
        if (inSubgraph[id]) subEdges.push_back(id);
    }

    sort(subEdges.begin(), subEdges.end(), [&](int x, int y) {
        if (edges[x].w != edges[y].w) return edges[x].w < edges[y].w;
        return x < y;
    });

    DSU dsuNode(n);
    vector<int> treeEdges;
    treeEdges.reserve(subEdges.size());

    for (int id : subEdges) {
        if (dsuNode.unite(edges[id].u, edges[id].v)) {
            treeEdges.push_back(id);
        }
    }

    int rootComp = dsuNode.find(terminals[0]);
    for (int t : terminals) {
        if (dsuNode.find(t) != rootComp) {
            cout << -1 << ' ' << 0 << '\n';
            return 0;
        }
    }

    vector<char> active(m, false);
    vector<vector<pair<int, int>>> treeAdj(n);
    vector<int> degree(n, 0);

    for (int id : treeEdges) {
        active[id] = true;
        int u = edges[id].u;
        int v = edges[id].v;
        treeAdj[u].push_back({v, id});
        treeAdj[v].push_back({u, id});
        ++degree[u];
        ++degree[v];
    }

    queue<int> q;
    for (int v = 0; v < n; ++v) {
        if (!isTerminal[v] && degree[v] == 1) {
            q.push(v);
        }
    }

    while (!q.empty()) {
        int v = q.front();
        q.pop();

        if (isTerminal[v] || degree[v] != 1) continue;

        int remEdge = -1;
        int nei = -1;
        for (const auto& [to, eid] : treeAdj[v]) {
            if (active[eid]) {
                remEdge = eid;
                nei = to;
                break;
            }
        }

        if (remEdge == -1) {
            degree[v] = 0;
            continue;
        }

        active[remEdge] = false;
        --degree[v];
        --degree[nei];

        if (!isTerminal[nei] && degree[nei] == 1) {
            q.push(nei);
        }
    }

    vector<int> finalEdges;
    finalEdges.reserve(treeEdges.size());
    long long totalWeight = 0;

    for (int id : treeEdges) {
        if (active[id]) {
            finalEdges.push_back(id);
            totalWeight += edges[id].w;
        }
    }

    DSU finalCheck(n);
    for (int id : finalEdges) {
        finalCheck.unite(edges[id].u, edges[id].v);
    }

    int r0 = finalCheck.find(terminals[0]);
    for (int t : terminals) {
        if (finalCheck.find(t) != r0) {
            cout << -1 << ' ' << 0 << '\n';
            return 0;
        }
    }

    cout << totalWeight << ' ' << finalEdges.size() << '\n';
    for (int id : finalEdges) {
        cout << edges[id].u << ' ' << edges[id].v << '\n';
    }

    return 0;
}

View C++ Code (8228 chars)
#include <algorithm>
#include <cstdint>
#include <iostream>
#include <limits>
#include <numeric>
#include <queue>
#include <tuple>
#include <vector>

using namespace std;

struct Edge {
    int u;
    int v;
    long long w;
};

struct AdjEdge {
    int to;
    long long w;
    int id;
};

struct DSU {
    vector<int> p;
    vector<int> r;

    explicit DSU(int n = 0) {
        init(n);
    }

    void init(int n) {
        p.resize(n);
        r.assign(n, 0);
        iota(p.begin(), p.end(), 0);
    }

    int find(int x) {
        while (p[x] != x) {
            p[x] = p[p[x]];
            x = p[x];
        }
        return x;
    }

    bool unite(int a, int b) {
        a = find(a);
        b = find(b);
        if (a == b) return false;
        if (r[a] < r[b]) swap(a, b);
        p[b] = a;
        if (r[a] == r[b]) ++r[a];
        return true;
    }
};

struct Candidate {
    int a;
    int b;
    long long cost;
    int u;
    int v;
    int bridgeEdge;
};

static const long long INF = numeric_limits<long long>::max() / 4;

int main() {
    ios::sync_with_stdio(false);
    cin.tie(nullptr);

    int n, m, kInput;
    if (!(cin >> n >> m >> kInput)) {
        return 0;
    }

    vector<Edge> edges;
    edges.reserve(m);
    vector<vector<AdjEdge>> g(n);

    for (int i = 0; i < m; ++i) {
        int u, v;
        long long w;
        cin >> u >> v >> w;
        edges.push_back({u, v, w});
        g[u].push_back({v, w, i});
        g[v].push_back({u, w, i});
    }

    vector<int> terminals(kInput);
    for (int i = 0; i < kInput; ++i) {
        cin >> terminals[i];
    }

    sort(terminals.begin(), terminals.end());
    terminals.erase(unique(terminals.begin(), terminals.end()), terminals.end());
    const int k = static_cast<int>(terminals.size());

    if (k <= 1) {
        cout << 0 << ' ' << 0 << '\n';
        return 0;
    }

    vector<char> isTerminal(n, false);
    for (int t : terminals) isTerminal[t] = true;

    vector<long long> dist(n, INF);
    vector<int> owner(n, -1);
    vector<int> parent(n, -1);
    vector<int> parentEdge(n, -1);

    using State = tuple<long long, int, int>; // dist, owner, vertex
    priority_queue<State, vector<State>, greater<State>> pq;

    for (int i = 0; i < k; ++i) {
        int s = terminals[i];
        if (0 < dist[s] || (dist[s] == 0 && (owner[s] == -1 || i < owner[s]))) {
            dist[s] = 0;
            owner[s] = i;
            parent[s] = -1;
            parentEdge[s] = -1;
            pq.push({0, i, s});
        }
    }

    while (!pq.empty()) {
        auto [d, own, v] = pq.top();
        pq.pop();

        if (d != dist[v] || own != owner[v]) continue;

        for (const auto& e : g[v]) {
            if (d > INF - e.w) continue;
            long long nd = d + e.w;
            int to = e.to;
            if (nd < dist[to] || (nd == dist[to] && (owner[to] == -1 || own < owner[to]))) {
                dist[to] = nd;
                owner[to] = own;
                parent[to] = v;
                parentEdge[to] = e.id;
                pq.push({nd, own, to});
            }
        }
    }

    vector<Candidate> candidates;
    candidates.reserve(m);

    for (int id = 0; id < m; ++id) {
        int u = edges[id].u;
        int v = edges[id].v;
        int a = owner[u];
        int b = owner[v];
        if (a < 0 || b < 0 || a == b) continue;
        if (dist[u] >= INF || dist[v] >= INF) continue;

        int uu = u;
        int vv = v;
        if (a > b) {
            swap(a, b);
            swap(uu, vv);
        }

        long long cost = dist[uu] + edges[id].w + dist[vv];
        candidates.push_back({a, b, cost, uu, vv, id});
    }

    sort(candidates.begin(), candidates.end(), [](const Candidate& x, const Candidate& y) {
        if (x.a != y.a) return x.a < y.a;
        if (x.b != y.b) return x.b < y.b;
        if (x.cost != y.cost) return x.cost < y.cost;
        return x.bridgeEdge < y.bridgeEdge;
    });

    vector<Candidate> pairEdges;
    pairEdges.reserve(candidates.size());
    for (size_t i = 0; i < candidates.size();) {
        size_t j = i;
        Candidate best = candidates[i];
        while (j < candidates.size() && candidates[j].a == candidates[i].a && candidates[j].b == candidates[i].b) {
            if (candidates[j].cost < best.cost ||
                (candidates[j].cost == best.cost && candidates[j].bridgeEdge < best.bridgeEdge)) {
                best = candidates[j];
            }
            ++j;
        }
        pairEdges.push_back(best);
        i = j;
    }

    sort(pairEdges.begin(), pairEdges.end(), [](const Candidate& x, const Candidate& y) {
        if (x.cost != y.cost) return x.cost < y.cost;
        if (x.a != y.a) return x.a < y.a;
        if (x.b != y.b) return x.b < y.b;
        return x.bridgeEdge < y.bridgeEdge;
    });

    DSU dsuTerm(k);
    vector<Candidate> terminalMST;
    terminalMST.reserve(k - 1);

    for (const auto& c : pairEdges) {
        if (dsuTerm.unite(c.a, c.b)) {
            terminalMST.push_back(c);
            if (static_cast<int>(terminalMST.size()) == k - 1) break;
        }
    }

    if (static_cast<int>(terminalMST.size()) != k - 1) {
        cout << -1 << ' ' << 0 << '\n';
        return 0;
    }

    vector<char> inSubgraph(m, false);

    auto addPathToOwner = [&](int x) {
        while (x >= 0 && parent[x] != -1) {
            int eid = parentEdge[x];
            if (eid >= 0) inSubgraph[eid] = true;
            x = parent[x];
        }
    };

    for (const auto& c : terminalMST) {
        addPathToOwner(c.u);
        inSubgraph[c.bridgeEdge] = true;
        addPathToOwner(c.v);
    }

    vector<int> subEdges;
    subEdges.reserve(m);
    for (int id = 0; id < m; ++id) {
        if (inSubgraph[id]) subEdges.push_back(id);
    }

    sort(subEdges.begin(), subEdges.end(), [&](int x, int y) {
        if (edges[x].w != edges[y].w) return edges[x].w < edges[y].w;
        return x < y;
    });

    DSU dsuNode(n);
    vector<int> treeEdges;
    treeEdges.reserve(subEdges.size());

    for (int id : subEdges) {
        if (dsuNode.unite(edges[id].u, edges[id].v)) {
            treeEdges.push_back(id);
        }
    }

    int rootComp = dsuNode.find(terminals[0]);
    for (int t : terminals) {
        if (dsuNode.find(t) != rootComp) {
            cout << -1 << ' ' << 0 << '\n';
            return 0;
        }
    }

    vector<char> active(m, false);
    vector<vector<pair<int, int>>> treeAdj(n);
    vector<int> degree(n, 0);

    for (int id : treeEdges) {
        active[id] = true;
        int u = edges[id].u;
        int v = edges[id].v;
        treeAdj[u].push_back({v, id});
        treeAdj[v].push_back({u, id});
        ++degree[u];
        ++degree[v];
    }

    queue<int> q;
    for (int v = 0; v < n; ++v) {
        if (!isTerminal[v] && degree[v] == 1) {
            q.push(v);
        }
    }

    while (!q.empty()) {
        int v = q.front();
        q.pop();

        if (isTerminal[v] || degree[v] != 1) continue;

        int remEdge = -1;
        int nei = -1;
        for (const auto& [to, eid] : treeAdj[v]) {
            if (active[eid]) {
                remEdge = eid;
                nei = to;
                break;
            }
        }

        if (remEdge == -1) {
            degree[v] = 0;
            continue;
        }

        active[remEdge] = false;
        --degree[v];
        --degree[nei];

        if (!isTerminal[nei] && degree[nei] == 1) {
            q.push(nei);
        }
    }

    vector<int> finalEdges;
    finalEdges.reserve(treeEdges.size());
    long long totalWeight = 0;

    for (int id : treeEdges) {
        if (active[id]) {
            finalEdges.push_back(id);
            totalWeight += edges[id].w;
        }
    }

    DSU finalCheck(n);
    for (int id : finalEdges) {
        finalCheck.unite(edges[id].u, edges[id].v);
    }

    int r0 = finalCheck.find(terminals[0]);
    for (int t : terminals) {
        if (finalCheck.find(t) != r0) {
            cout << -1 << ' ' << 0 << '\n';
            return 0;
        }
    }

    cout << totalWeight << ' ' << finalEdges.size() << '\n';
    for (int id : finalEdges) {
        cout << edges[id].u << ' ' << edges[id].v << '\n';
    }

    return 0;
}

Skipped due to earlier zero score
Test 33: Quadratic Assignment Problem (C#) Score: 0.00 / 17
Typical Prompt:
You are writing C# code to solve the Quadratic Assignment Problem (QAP), which is NP-hard.

**Problem:**
You have N facilities and N locations. You must assign each facility to exactly one
location (a permutation). The cost of assigning facility i to location p[i] is:

sum over all pairs (i, j): flow[i][j] * distance[p[i]][p[j]]

Your goal is to minimize this total cost.

**Input format (stdin):**
```
N
flow[0][0] flow[0][1] ... flow[0][N-1]
...
flow[N-1][0] ... flow[N-1][N-1]
dist[0][0] dist[0][1] ... dist[0][N-1]
...
dist[N-1][0] ... dist[N-1][N-1]
```
All values are integers. Matrices are N×N. Indices are 0-based in the output.

**Output format (stdout):**
```
total_cost
p0 p1 p2 ... p(N-1)
```
Where p is a permutation of 0..N-1 and p[i] is the location assigned to facility i.

**Requirements:**
1. Read from stdin, write to stdout.
2. Output a valid permutation.
3. Handle N from very small up to ~2000 within 5 minutes.
4. Heuristics are acceptable for large N; lower cost scores higher.

Write complete, compilable C# code with a Main method. Assume only the standard library.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Assign N facilities to N locations so that the total cost is minimised, where cost depends on how much material flows between each pair of facilities multiplied by the distance between their assigned locations. Think of arranging departments in an office to minimise how far people walk between them.

This is the Quadratic Assignment Problem, one of the hardest combinatorial optimisation problems. The test uses planted instances with a known optimum for strict grading. Subpasses increase the problem size.

Test 34: Vehicle Routing Problem (Python) Score: 0.00 / 17
Typical Prompt:
You are writing Python code to solve the Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP).

**Problem:**
You have a depot and N customers with coordinates and demands. You must create up to K vehicle
routes starting and ending at the depot. Each customer must be visited exactly once and the sum
of demands on any route must not exceed the vehicle capacity. Minimize total travel distance.

**Input format (stdin):**
```
N K CAPACITY
depot_x depot_y
x1 y1 demand1
x2 y2 demand2
...
xN yN demandN
```

**Output format (stdout):**
```
total_distance
num_routes
route_1
route_2
...
```
Each route line should list customer indices (0-based) in visit order.

**Requirements:**
1. Read from stdin, write to stdout.
2. Output valid routes that cover all customers exactly once.
3. Respect vehicle capacity on every route.
4. Heuristics are acceptable for large instances; shorter distance scores higher.

Write complete, runnable Python code.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Plan delivery routes for a fleet of vehicles so that every customer is visited and the total distance travelled is minimised. Each vehicle has a capacity limit, so the AI must decide which customers go on which truck and in what order.

This is the Vehicle Routing Problem, a generalisation of the Travelling Salesman Problem. Subpasses increase the number of customers and vehicles. The baseline uses a simple nearest-neighbour heuristic.

Test 35: Minimum Dominating Set (Rust) Score: 0.00 / 21
Typical Prompt:
You are writing Rust code to solve the Minimum Dominating Set problem (NP-hard).

**Problem:**
Given an undirected graph, find a small set of vertices S such that every vertex is
either in S or adjacent to at least one vertex in S. Smaller sets score better.

**Input format (stdin):**
```
num_vertices num_edges
u1 v1
u2 v2
...
```
Vertices are 0-indexed.

**Output format (stdout):**
```
set_size
v1 v2 v3 ...
```
Where the second line lists the vertices in the dominating set (0-indexed).

**Requirements:**
1. Read from stdin, write to stdout.
2. Output a valid dominating set.
3. Heuristics are acceptable for large graphs; smaller sets score higher.
4. Must run within 5 minutes.

**Target environment specs**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**Rust Compiler information**
rustc 1.92.0 (ded5c06cf 2025-12-08) | edition=2024 | host: x86_64-pc-windows-msvc

Test 36: Maximum Independent Set (C++) Score: 0.00 / 13
Typical Prompt:
You are writing C++ code to solve the **Maximum Independent Set** problem.

Given an undirected graph, find the largest set of vertices such that no two chosen vertices share an edge.

The evaluator is strict: only an exact maximum independent set is accepted.

**Input format (stdin)**
```
n m
u v
u v
... (m lines total)
```
- `n` = number of vertices (0-indexed: 0..n-1)
- `m` = number of undirected edges
- each edge appears as two integers `u v`

**Output format (stdout)**
```
k
v1 v2 v3 ... vk
```
- `k` = size of your independent set
- second line lists exactly `k` distinct vertex indices (space-separated)
- vertices may be printed in any order

**What will be checked**
1. Output format is valid.
2. Every printed vertex is in range.
3. No edge exists between any two printed vertices.
4. `k` is the true maximum for the input graph.

**Requirements**
1. Use standard C++ (C++17-compatible).
2. Read from stdin and write to stdout.
3. Handle small and very large graphs.
4. Finish within the test timeout.

**Environment**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C++ Compiler**
MSVC cl 19.50.35728 | std=c++20 (/std:c++20)

This is NP-hard. The test uses planted instances with a known optimal answer for strict pass/fail grading. Subpasses increase the graph size.

Test 37: Feedback Vertex Set (C#) Score: 0.00 / 14
Typical Prompt:
You are writing C# code to solve the **Directed Feedback Vertex Set** problem.

Given a directed graph, find the smallest set of vertices to remove so the remaining graph has **no directed cycles** (i.e., it becomes a DAG).

The evaluator is strict: only a minimum-size valid feedback vertex set is accepted.

**Input format (stdin)**
```
n m
u v
u v
... (m lines total)
```
- `n`: number of vertices (0-indexed: `0..n-1`)
- `m`: number of directed edges
- each line `u v` means an edge `u -> v`

**Output format (stdout)**
```
k
v1 v2 v3 ... vk
```
- `k`: number of removed vertices
- second line: exactly `k` distinct vertex indices (any order)

**What is checked**
1. Output format is valid.
2. Vertex indices are valid and distinct.
3. Removing those vertices makes the graph acyclic.
4. The set size is minimum.

**Requirements**
1. Provide complete C# code with `Main`.
2. Read from stdin and write to stdout.
3. Handle small and very large graphs.
4. Finish within the test timeout.

**Environment**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: Intel64 Family 6 Model 158 Stepping 10, GenuineIntel
Core count: 12
Memory: 31.93 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

**C# Compiler**

Find the smallest set of nodes to remove from a directed graph so that no cycles remain. Think of breaking deadlocks in a dependency chain — remove as few tasks as possible so that the rest can be completed in a straight order.

This is NP-hard. The test uses planted instances where a known small set of nodes creates all the cycles, enabling strict pass/fail grading. Subpasses increase the graph size.

Test 38: Exact Cover (Python) Score: 0.00 / 16
Typical Prompt:
You are writing Python code to solve the **Exact Cover** problem.

Given a universe of elements and a list of sets, choose set indices such that:
1) every element is covered, and
2) no element is covered more than once.

The evaluator is strict: only the exact expected solution is accepted.

**Input format (stdin)**
```
U S
e1 e2 e3 ...
e1 e2 ...
... (S lines total)
```
- `U` = number of elements in the universe (elements are `0..U-1`)
- `S` = number of available sets
- each following line is one set, listed as space-separated element IDs

**Output format (stdout)**
```
SOLUTION
i1 i2 i3 ... ik
```
or
```
NO SOLUTION
```

- `i1..ik` are 0-based set indices into the input list.

**What is checked**
1. Output format is valid.
2. Indices are valid and distinct.
3. Chosen indices are exactly the planted exact-cover solution.

Write complete Python code that reads stdin and writes stdout.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Given a collection of sets, choose some of them so that every element in the universe appears in exactly one chosen set — no gaps and no overlaps. Think of tiling a floor with pre-cut pieces where every tile must be used exactly once and every square must be covered.

This is a classic NP-complete problem. Each test instance has exactly one valid solution, so grading is strict pass/fail. Subpasses increase the universe and set sizes.

Test 39: Graph Bisection (Rust) Score: 0.00 / 13
Typical Prompt:
You are writing Rust code to solve the **Graph Bisection** problem.

Given an undirected graph with an even number of vertices, split the vertices into two equal-size parts to minimize cut edges (edges with endpoints in different parts).

The evaluator is strict: only a minimum-cut valid bisection is accepted.

**Input format (stdin)**
```
n m
u v
u v
... (m lines total)
```
- `n` vertices labeled `0..n-1` (n is even)
- `m` undirected edges

**Output format (stdout)**
```
cut_size
v1 v2 v3 ... v(n/2)
```
- first line: reported cut size
- second line: vertices in partition A (exactly n/2 distinct indices)
- partition B is all remaining vertices

**What is checked**
1. Output format is valid.
2. Partition size is exactly `n/2` and vertices are valid/distinct.
3. Reported cut matches the actual cut.
4. The cut is minimum for the instance.

Write complete Rust code with `main` that reads stdin and writes stdout.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Split a graph’s nodes into two equally-sized groups so that as few edges as possible cross between the groups. Think of dividing a school into two teams where friends want to stay together — minimise the number of friendships that get split.

The test uses planted graphs that can be perfectly bisected with zero crossing edges, so grading is strict pass/fail. Subpasses increase the graph size.

Test 40: Integer Linear Programming (C++) Score: 0.00 / 14
Typical Prompt:
You are writing C++ code to solve a **0/1 Integer Linear Program**.

Maximize:
sum(c_i * x_i)

Subject to:
x_u + x_v <= 1 (for each constraint pair u, v)
x_i in {0, 1}

**Input format (stdin)**
```
n m
c_0 c_1 ... c_(n-1)
u_0 v_0
u_1 v_1
... (m lines total)
```
- `n` = number of binary variables
- `m` = number of pair constraints

**Output format (stdout)**
```
objective_value
x_0 x_1 ... x_(n-1)
```

**What is checked**
1. Output format is valid.
2. `x_i` are binary and satisfy all constraints.
3. Reported objective matches the assignment.
4. Assignment achieves the true optimum for the instance.

Write complete C++17 code with `main()` that reads stdin and writes stdout.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Maximise a score by choosing which items to switch “on” or “off”, subject to a set of rules that restrict certain combinations. Each item has a value, and the rules say things like “you can’t pick both A and B”.

This is 0/1 Integer Linear Programming, a fundamental NP-hard optimisation problem. The test uses planted instances with a known unique optimum for strict pass/fail grading. Subpasses increase the number of variables and constraints.

Test 41: Fragment Shaders (SPIR-V Assembly) Score: 0.00 / 20
Typical Prompt:
You are writing a SPIR-V assembly text fragment shader. The shader will be assembled
with spirv-as (Vulkan 1.0 target) and rendered on a unit sphere.

**Available inputs (from vertex shader):**
```
layout(location = 0) in vec3 worldPos; ; world-space position
layout(location = 1) in vec3 normal; ; world-space normal (interpolated, may need normalize)
layout(location = 2) in vec2 uv; ; texture coordinates in [0,1]
layout(location = 3) in vec3 tangent; ; tangent vector
layout(location = 4) in vec3 color; ; per-vertex color (smoothly varies: typical value: rgb = 0.5+0.5*xyz)
```

**Available uniform buffer (DescriptorSet 0, Binding 0):**
```
struct UBO { // std140 layout
mat4 model; // offset 0 (column-major)
mat4 view; // offset 64
mat4 projection; // offset 128
vec4 lightPos; // offset 192 -- typical value: (5.0, 5.0, 5.0, 1.0)
vec4 cameraPos; // offset 208 -- typical value: (0.0, 0.0, 3.0, 1.0)
vec4 params; // offset 224 -- typical value: (0.0, 0.5, 0.0, 0.0)
};
```

**Required output:**
```
layout(location = 0) out vec4 outColor; RGBA float, will be stored as rgba8unorm
```

**SPIR-V requirements:**
- Start with `; SPIR-V` header comment and version
- Use `OpCapability Shader` and `OpMemoryModel Logical GLSL450`
- Use `OpEntryPoint Fragment %main "main"` listing ALL input/output variables used
- Use `OpExecutionMode %main OriginUpperLeft`
- Declare all necessary decorations (Location, DescriptorSet, Binding, Block, Offsets)
- All input variables are `Input` storage class, output is `Output` storage class
- The uniform buffer variable is `Uniform` storage class
- Entry point function name must be "main"

**Write ONLY the complete SPIR-V assembly text.** Do not include any other text or explanation
outside the SPIR-V text.


**Task:** Output a solid red color (vec4(1.0, 0.0, 0.0, 1.0)).
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output

Write raw SPIR-V assembly for Vulkan fragment shaders. The task is to reproduce a series of target visual effects on a shaded sphere: simple colors, instruction set that graphics cards actually execute. Each subpass asks for a different visual effect (lighting, patterns, fractals) rendered onto a 3D sphere, and the output image is compared pixel-by-pixel against a reference.

This is the hardest shader-language test because SPIR-V assembly is essentially GPU machine code: every operation, type declaration, and memory access must be

Test 42: Geometry Shaders (SPIR-V Assembly) Score: 0.00 / 15
Typical Prompt:
You are writing a SPIR-V assembly geometry shader. It will be assembled with
spirv-as (Vulkan 1.0) and rendered using a fixed vertex shader and fragment shader.

**Geometry shader requirements:**
- `OpCapability Geometry`
- `OpMemoryModel Logical GLSL450`
- `OpEntryPoint Geometry %main "main"` listing ALL interface variables
- `OpExecutionMode %main ` (InputPoints/InputLines/Triangles/InputLinesAdjacency/InputTrianglesAdjacency)
- `OpExecutionMode %main ` (OutputPoints/OutputLineStrip/OutputTriangleStrip)
- `OpExecutionMode %main OutputVertices ` (max vertices emitted)
- `OpExecutionMode %main Invocations 1`

**Input from vertex shader (per-vertex arrays):**
The input gl_Position is in a gl_PerVertex block (must be decorated with Block + BuiltIn Position).
```
%gl_PerVertex_in = OpTypeStruct %v4float
OpDecorate %gl_PerVertex_in Block
OpMemberDecorate %gl_PerVertex_in 0 BuiltIn Position
; Array size depends on input primitive:
; InputPoints: 1, InputLines: 2, Triangles: 3
; InputLinesAdjacency: 4, InputTrianglesAdjacency: 6
%arr_pv_in = OpTypeArray %gl_PerVertex_in %uint_N
%ptr_in_arr_pv = OpTypePointer Input %arr_pv_in
%gl_in = OpVariable %ptr_in_arr_pv Input
; Access: OpAccessChain %ptr_in_v4 %gl_in %int_i %int_0 -> gl_in[i].gl_Position
```

User-defined inputs are also arrays of the same size:
```
layout(location = 0) in vec3 worldPos[]; ; positions in clip space
layout(location = 1) in vec3 normal[]; ; vertex normals
layout(location = 2) in vec3 color[]; ; per-vertex colors
```

**Output (per emitted vertex):**
The output gl_Position is in a gl_PerVertex block:
```
%gl_PerVertex_out = OpTypeStruct %v4float
OpDecorate %gl_PerVertex_out Block
OpMemberDecorate %gl_PerVertex_out 0 BuiltIn Position
%ptr_out_pv = OpTypePointer Output %gl_PerVertex_out
%gl_out = OpVariable %ptr_out_pv Output
; Store: OpAccessChain %ptr_out_v4 %gl_out %int_0 -> gl_out.gl_Position
```
```
layout(location = 0) out vec3 outColor; ; color for fragment shader
```

**Emit vertices with:** `OpEmitVertex` after storing gl_Position and outColor.
**End primitive with:** `OpEndPrimitive` after emitting a complete strip.

**Available UBO (set=0, binding=0) - accessible from geometry shader:**
Same layout as test 41 (model/view/proj matrices, lightPos, cameraPos, params).

**Write ONLY the complete SPIR-V assembly text for the geometry shader.**


**Input primitive:** InputPoints (1 vertex per invocation, array size 1)
**Geometry:** 25 colored points in a 5x5 grid spread across [-0.8, 0.8].

**Task:** Expand each point into a small colored quad (4 vertices as triangle strip).
1. Read gl_in[0].gl_Position (the point position).
2. Read color[0] (the point's color).
3. Define a half-size: h = 0.04.
4. Emit 4 vertices forming a quad centered on the point:
- (x-h, y-h), (x+h, y-h), (x-h, y+h), (x+h, y+h)
- All with z=0, w=1, and the same color.
5. Output as triangle strip, max 4 vertices.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Write GPU geometry shaders in raw SPIR-V assembly. Geometry shaders receive primitives (points, lines, or triangles) and can output completely new geometry — for example, turning a single point into a billboard quad or a thin line into a thick ribbon.

Each subpass specifies a different input primitive type and a transformation task. The rendered output is compared pixel-by-pixel against a reference image. Writing geometry shaders in raw SPIR-V assembly requires precise control of vertex emission and primitive topology.

Test 43: Fragment Shaders (HLSL) Score: 0.00 / 20
Typical Prompt:
You are writing an HLSL pixel (fragment) shader for Vulkan. The shader will be
compiled with glslangValidator (-D flag for HLSL mode) targeting Vulkan 1.0,
then rendered on a unit sphere.

**Available inputs (from vertex shader, using Vulkan layout attributes):**
```hlsl
struct PSInput {
[[vk::location(0)]] float3 worldPos : TEXCOORD0; // world-space position
[[vk::location(1)]] float3 normal : TEXCOORD1; // world-space normal (interpolated, may need normalize)
[[vk::location(2)]] float2 uv : TEXCOORD2; // texture coordinates in [0,1]
[[vk::location(3)]] float3 tangent : TEXCOORD3; // tangent vector
[[vk::location(4)]] float3 color : TEXCOORD4; // per-vertex color (smoothly varies: rgb = 0.5+0.5*xyz)
};
```

**Available constant buffer (set 0, binding 0):**
```hlsl
[[vk::binding(0, 0)]]
cbuffer UBO : register(b0) {
float4x4 model; // offset 0 (column-major)
float4x4 view; // offset 64
float4x4 projection; // offset 128
float4 lightPos; // value: (5.0, 5.0, 5.0, 1.0)
float4 cameraPos; // value: (0.0, 0.0, 3.0, 1.0)
float4 params; // value: (0.0, 0.5, 0.0, 0.0)
};
```

**Required output:**
```hlsl
float4 main(PSInput input) : SV_Target0 // RGBA float, stored as rgba8unorm
```

**HLSL requirements:**
- Use Vulkan-specific attributes: `[[vk::location(N)]]` for inputs, `[[vk::binding(B, S)]]` for UBO
- Entry point must be named `main`
- Return type is `float4` with `SV_Target0` semantic
- Standard HLSL intrinsics are available: normalize, dot, reflect, pow, max, clamp, lerp, etc.
- Use `mul()` for matrix-vector operations if needed

**Write ONLY the complete HLSL source code.** Do not include any other text or explanation
outside the shader source.


**Task:** Output a solid red color (float4(1.0, 0.0, 0.0, 1.0)).

The code is compiled to SPIR-V behind the scenes and rendered; the output is compared pixel-by-pixel against the reference images.

Test 44: Fragment Shaders (GLSL) Score: 0.00 / 20
Typical Prompt:
You are writing a GLSL fragment shader for Vulkan (GLSL 450). The shader will be
compiled with glslangValidator targeting Vulkan 1.0, then rendered on a unit sphere.

**Available inputs (from vertex shader):**
```glsl
layout(location = 0) in vec3 worldPos; // world-space position
layout(location = 1) in vec3 normal; // world-space normal (interpolated, may need normalize)
layout(location = 2) in vec2 uv; // texture coordinates in [0,1]
layout(location = 3) in vec3 tangent; // tangent vector
layout(location = 4) in vec3 color; // per-vertex color (smoothly varies: rgb = 0.5+0.5*xyz)
```

**Available uniform buffer (set 0, binding 0):**
```glsl
layout(set = 0, binding = 0) uniform UBO {
mat4 model; // offset 0 (column-major)
mat4 view; // offset 64
mat4 projection; // offset 128
vec4 lightPos; // value: (5.0, 5.0, 5.0, 1.0)
vec4 cameraPos; // value: (0.0, 0.0, 3.0, 1.0)
vec4 params; // value: (0.0, 0.5, 0.0, 0.0)
} ubo;
```

**Required output:**
```glsl
layout(location = 0) out vec4 outColor; // RGBA float, stored as rgba8unorm
```

**GLSL requirements:**
- Must start with `#version 450`
- Use `layout(location = N)` for inputs/outputs
- Use `layout(set = 0, binding = 0)` for the UBO
- Standard GLSL built-ins available: normalize, dot, reflect, pow, max, clamp, mix, etc.
- Entry point must be `void main()`

**Write ONLY the complete GLSL source code.** Do not include any other text or explanation
outside the shader source.


**Task:** Output a solid red color (vec4(1.0, 0.0, 0.0, 1.0)).

Test 45: Fragment Shaders (SPIRV) Score: 0.00 / 20
Typical Prompt:
You are writing a SPIR-V fragment shader as raw binary data, encoded as a hex string.
The binary will be decoded from hex, validated with spirv-val, and rendered on a unit sphere.

**SPIR-V Binary Format (little-endian 32-bit words):**
Each 32-bit word is 8 hex characters (little-endian byte order).

Header (5 words):
```
03022307 // Magic number 0x07230203 (little-endian)
00000100 // Version 1.0 (0x00010000)
00000000 // Generator (0 = manual)
BBBBBBBB // Bound (max ID + 1)
00000000 // Reserved
```

Instructions follow as: first word = (WordCount << 16) | Opcode, then operands.

**Key opcodes (decimal):**
- OpCapability (17): word_count=2, operand=capability (1=Shader)
- OpExtInstImport (11): result_id, name_string
- OpMemoryModel (14): word_count=3, addressing=0(Logical), model=1(GLSL450)
- OpEntryPoint (15): execution_model=4(Fragment), entry_id, name, interface_vars...
- OpExecutionMode (16): entry_id, mode=7(OriginUpperLeft)
- OpDecorate (71): target, decoration, operands
- OpTypeVoid (19), OpTypeFunction (33), OpTypeFloat (22), OpTypeVector (23)
- OpTypePointer (32), OpVariable (59), OpConstant (43)
- OpFunction (54), OpFunctionEnd (56), OpLabel (248)
- OpLoad (61), OpStore (62), OpReturn (253)
- OpCompositeConstruct (80), OpCompositeExtract (81)
- OpDot (148), OpFMul (133), OpFAdd (129), OpFSub (131), OpFNegate (127)
- OpExtInst (12): GLSL.std.450 ext instructions

**Decoration values:** Location=30, DescriptorSet=34, Binding=33, Block=2, Offset=35

**Available inputs (from vertex shader):**
```
location 0: vec3 worldPos
location 1: vec3 normal
location 2: vec2 uv
location 3: vec3 tangent
location 4: vec3 color
```

**UBO (set=0, binding=0):** mat4 model(0), mat4 view(64), mat4 projection(128),
vec4 lightPos(192), vec4 cameraPos(208), vec4 params(224).

**Required output:** location 0: vec4 outColor

**Write ONLY the complete hex string of the SPIR-V binary.** Each byte as two hex
characters, no spaces or newlines within the hex data. The hex string represents
the raw bytes of the .spv file.


**Task:** Output a solid red color (vec4(1.0, 0.0, 0.0, 1.0)).

This is the most extreme shader test: every opcode, operand, and ID must be numerically correct at the byte level. The binary is validated, rendered on a sphere, and compared pixel-by-pixel against the reference images.

Test 46: N-Body Gravitational Simulation (GLSL Compute) Score: 0.00 / 6
Typical Prompt:
Write a GLSL compute shader for gravitational N-body simulation.

**GLSL Compute Shader Interface:**

```
#version 450

layout(local_size_x = 256) in;

// Each body is stored as two consecutive vec4s:
// vec4(position.xyz, mass)
// vec4(velocity.xyz, 0.0)
// So body i starts at index i*2 in the buffer.

layout(set = 0, binding = 0) readonly buffer InputBodies {
vec4 inData[]; // length = N * 2
};

layout(set = 0, binding = 1) buffer OutputBodies {
vec4 outData[]; // length = N * 2
};

layout(set = 0, binding = 2) uniform Params {
uvec4 counts; // counts.x = N (number of bodies)
vec4 physics; // physics.x = dt, physics.y = softening, physics.z = G
};
```

**Task:** Write a compute shader that performs ONE timestep of gravitational
N-body simulation using the leapfrog/Euler integration method. Each invocation
handles one body (gl_GlobalInvocationID.x), reading its position and velocity
from inData and writing updated values to outData. The acceleration should be
computed from all other bodies using:

acc += G * mass_j * (pos_j - pos_i) / (|pos_j - pos_i|^2 + softening^2)^(3/2)

Velocity is advanced by acc * dt and position is advanced by vel * dt. The
shader will be dispatched ceil(N/256) workgroups in X and run multiple times
with ping-pong buffers for the required number of timesteps.

**Critical Requirements:**
- Guard against gl_GlobalInvocationID.x >= N
- Use the softening parameter to avoid singularities
- Skip self-interaction (i == j)
- Write BOTH position+mass and velocity vec4s to output


**Test Configurations:**
Subpass 0: N=64, timesteps=10, dt=0.001, softening=0.01
Subpass 1: N=128, timesteps=20, dt=0.001, softening=0.01
Subpass 2: N=256, timesteps=50, dt=0.001, softening=0.01
Subpass 3: N=512, timesteps=100, dt=0.001, softening=0.01
Subpass 4: N=1024, timesteps=100, dt=0.001, softening=0.01
Subpass 5: N=2048, timesteps=200, dt=0.001, softening=0.01

G = 0.006674 (scaled gravitational constant)

Write the complete GLSL compute shader source code.

Subpasses increase the number of bodies (up to thousands) and timesteps, testing both correctness and numerical stability. Errors accumulate over time, so even small inaccuracies compound into visible drift.

Test 47: Hash Mining / Proof of Work (WGSL Compute) Score: 0.00 / 5
Typical Prompt:
Write a WGSL compute shader for hash mining (proof-of-work).

**WGSL Compute Shader Interface:**

```wgsl
@group(0) @binding(0) var baseData : array;
@group(0) @binding(1) var result : array;
@group(0) @binding(2) var params : vec4;
// params.x = difficulty, params.y = range_start, params.z = range_size

@compute @workgroup_size(256)
fn main(@builtin(global_invocation_id) gid : vec3) { ... }
```

**Task:** Write a compute shader that searches for a uint32 nonce such that
the custom hash of (baseData XOR nonce) has at least `difficulty` trailing
zero bits in the 64-bit output.

**Hash function (MUST match exactly):**
```
function custom_hash(data[16], nonce) -> (h0, h1):
state[0..15] = data[0..15]
for i in 0..15: state[i] ^= (nonce + i)

// 8 rounds of quarter-round mixing (ChaCha-like)
repeat 8 times:
for each group of 4 consecutive elements (i, i+1, i+2, i+3):
a += b; d ^= a; d = rotl(d, 16)
c += d; b ^= c; b = rotl(b, 12)
a += b; d ^= a; d = rotl(d, 8)
c += d; b ^= c; b = rotl(b, 7)

// Fold 16 words to 2:
h0 = state[0] ^ state[2] ^ state[4] ^ state[6] ^ state[8] ^ state[10] ^ state[12] ^ state[14]
h1 = state[1] ^ state[3] ^ state[5] ^ state[7] ^ state[9] ^ state[11] ^ state[13] ^ state[15]
return (h0, h1) // h0 is low 32 bits
```

**Trailing zero count:** Count trailing zero bits of the 64-bit value [h0 | (h1 << 32)].
That is, count from h0's LSB first; if h0 == 0, add 32 and continue counting from h1.

**Algorithm:**
1. Each thread computes: my_nonce = params.y + gid.x (range_start + thread ID)
2. Guard: if gid.x >= params.z, return (out of range)
3. Compute hash, count trailing zeros
4. If trailing_zeros >= difficulty:
Write result[0]=1u, result[1]=nonce, result[2]=h0, result[3]=h1
(Race writes are acceptable - any valid nonce will be verified on CPU)

**Important WGSL notes:**
- Use `var state : array` for the mutable hash state.
- WGSL rotation: `(x << shift) | (x >> (32u - shift))` — all values must be u32.
- The inner quarter-round loop MUST be unrolled (write out all 4 groups
of state[0..3], state[4..7], state[8..11], state[12..15] explicitly).
- Do NOT use atomics. Plain stores to result[] are sufficient.
- All integer literals must be typed: use `0u`, `1u`, `16u`, `32u` etc.

The shader will be dispatched with ceil(range_size / 256) workgroups.
Only ONE dispatch is performed - find the nonce in a single pass.


**Test Configurations:**
Subpass 0: difficulty=4 bits, search_range=65,536
Subpass 1: difficulty=8 bits, search_range=1,048,576
Subpass 2: difficulty=12 bits, search_range=16,777,216
Subpass 3: difficulty=16 bits, search_range=268,435,456
Subpass 4: difficulty=20 bits, search_range=1,073,741,824

Write the complete WGSL compute shader source code.
The shader must implement the exact hash function described above.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0

Subpasses increase the difficulty (more trailing zeros required), demanding that the shader correctly implement bitwise hashing and parallel search. The found nonce is verified on the CPU.

Test 48: 2D Fluid Simulation - Lattice Boltzmann (SPIR-V ASM Compute) Score: 0.00 / 5
Typical Prompt:
Write a SPIR-V assembly compute shader for 2D Lattice Boltzmann fluid simulation.

**SPIR-V Assembly Compute Shader Interface:**

The shader uses the D2Q9 Lattice Boltzmann model for 2D fluid simulation.

**D2Q9 velocity directions (index k -> (cx, cy)):**
```
k=0: ( 0, 0) w=4/9 (rest)
k=1: ( 1, 0) w=1/9 (east)
k=2: ( 0, 1) w=1/9 (north)
k=3: (-1, 0) w=1/9 (west)
k=4: ( 0,-1) w=1/9 (south)
k=5: ( 1, 1) w=1/36 (NE)
k=6: (-1, 1) w=1/36 (NW)
k=7: (-1,-1) w=1/36 (SW)
k=8: ( 1,-1) w=1/36 (SE)
```

**Buffer layout:**
- Binding 0 (storage, read-only): float array, length W*H*9
Index = (y * W + x) * 9 + k
- Binding 1 (storage, read-write): float array, length W*H*9 (output, NOT writeonly)
- Binding 2 (uniform): { uvec4(W, H, 0, 0), vec4(omega, 0, 0, 0) }

**Algorithm (per cell at gl_GlobalInvocationID.xy):**
Compute one LBM step (collision + streaming) for each lattice cell. Guard against
out-of-bounds (x >= W or y >= H). For the cell distributions f[0..8], compute
macroscopic density rho and velocity (ux, uy) using the D2Q9 directions. Apply
the BGK collision update with:

cu = cx[k]*ux + cy[k]*uy
feq = w[k] * rho * (1 + 3*cu + 4.5*cu*cu - 1.5*(ux*ux + uy*uy))
f_out = f[k] + omega * (feq - f[k])

Then stream each f_out to the neighbor cell (x+cx[k], y+cy[k]) with periodic
wraparound in both axes. Write to the output buffer at the corresponding
neighbor index for k.

**SPIR-V requirements:**
- OpCapability Shader
- OpExecutionMode with LocalSize 16 16 1
- Use gl_GlobalInvocationID for (x, y)
- Declare runtime arrays for storage buffers with OpDecorate ArrayStride 4
- Use OpSMod or manual modulo for periodic boundary conditions


**Test Configurations:**
Subpass 0: 32x32 grid, 50 steps, viscosity=0.1, omega=1.2500
Subpass 1: 64x64 grid, 100 steps, viscosity=0.1, omega=1.2500
Subpass 2: 128x128 grid, 100 steps, viscosity=0.05, omega=1.5385
Subpass 3: 128x128 grid, 200 steps, viscosity=0.05, omega=1.5385
Subpass 4: 256x256 grid, 200 steps, viscosity=0.02, omega=1.7857

Write the complete SPIR-V assembly text (starting with "; SPIR-V").
The shader performs ONE LBM step (collision + streaming). It will be
dispatched repeatedly for the required number of timesteps with ping-pong buffers.

The final grid state is compared against a CPU reference. Subpasses increase the grid resolution and timestep count, testing both correctness and numerical stability of the simulation.

Test 49: Parallel Prefix Sum (SPIR-V Binary Compute) Score: 0.00 / 5
Typical Prompt:
Write a SPIR-V binary compute shader (hex-encoded) for parallel inclusive prefix sum.

**SPIR-V Binary (hex-encoded) Compute Shader Interface:**

You must produce raw SPIR-V binary encoded as a hex string (no spaces, no 0x prefix).
The binary must start with the SPIR-V magic number 0x07230203.

**Required layout:**
```
Binding 0 (set=0): Storage buffer (read-only) - uint array, length N
Binding 1 (set=0): Storage buffer (read-write) - uint array, length N
Binding 2 (set=0): Uniform buffer - uint4: [N, 0, 0, 0]

Local size: 256 x 1 x 1
```

**Algorithm:** Inclusive prefix sum (scan)
- Input: [a0, a1, a2, a3, ...]
- Output: [a0, a0+a1, a0+a1+a2, a0+a1+a2+a3, ...]

Compute an inclusive scan over the input array. For N <= 256, use a shared-memory
scan within a single workgroup (synchronize as needed). For N > 256, a naive
fallback (each thread sums all previous elements) is acceptable but slow; full
marks require a multi-workgroup scan.

**SPIR-V binary encoding notes:**
- Little-endian uint32 words
- Magic: 0x07230203
- Version: 0x00010000 (1.0)
- Bound: upper bound on all IDs used + 1
- Schema: 0
- OpCapability Shader = [0x00020011, 0x00000001]
- OpMemoryModel Logical GLSL450 = [0x00030006, 0x00000001, 0x00000001]
- OpEntryPoint GLCompute = opcode 0x0000000F, execution model 5
- OpExecutionMode LocalSize = opcode 0x00000010, mode 17 (LocalSize)
- OpDecorate Binding = decoration 33
- OpDecorate DescriptorSet = decoration 34
- OpDecorate ArrayStride = decoration 6
- OpDecorate BufferBlock or Block = decoration 2 or 3
- OpTypeInt 32 0 = unsigned int
- OpTypePointer StorageBuffer = storage class 12
- OpVariable StorageBuffer = storage class 12

Encode every uint32 word as exactly 8 hex characters (little-endian).


**Test Configurations:**
Subpass 0: N=256 elements
Subpass 1: N=1,024 elements
Subpass 2: N=4,096 elements
Subpass 3: N=16,384 elements
Subpass 4: N=65,536 elements

Provide the complete SPIR-V binary as a hex string.
Each uint32 word must be 8 hex digits, little-endian byte order.
The entire binary is one continuous hex string with no spaces or line breaks.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Write a GPU compute shader as raw SPIR-V binary that computes a parallel prefix sum (inclusive scan) of an array of integers. Given an input like [3, 1, 4, 1, 5], the output should be [3, 4, 8, 9, 14] — each element is the sum of all elements up to and including that position.

Doing this efficiently on a GPU requires a multi-pass tree-based algorithm, not a simple loop. Subpasses increase the array size up to tens of thousands of elements. The result must match the CPU reference exactly, element by element.

Test 50: Boid Flocking Simulation (GLSL Compute) Score: 0.00 / 6
Typical Prompt:
Write a GLSL compute shader for 3D boid flocking simulation.

**GLSL Compute Shader Interface:**

```glsl
#version 450

layout(local_size_x = 256) in;

// Each boid stored as two vec4s: [pos.xyz, 0], [vel.xyz, 0]
// Boid i is at index i*2 and i*2+1
layout(set = 0, binding = 0) readonly buffer InputBoids {
vec4 inData[]; // length = N * 2
};

layout(set = 0, binding = 1) buffer OutputBoids {
vec4 outData[]; // length = N * 2
};

layout(set = 0, binding = 2) uniform Params {
uvec4 counts; // counts.x = N
vec4 radii; // radii.x=dt, radii.y=sepRadius, radii.z=alignRadius, radii.w=cohRadius
vec4 weights; // weights.x=sepWeight, weights.y=alignWeight, weights.z=cohWeight, weights.w=maxSpeed
vec4 bounds; // bounds.x=boundSize, bounds.y=boundForce
};
```

**Task:** Write a compute shader that performs ONE timestep of 3D boid
flocking simulation (Reynolds' rules). Each invocation handles one boid
(gl_GlobalInvocationID.x), reading its position/velocity from inData and
writing updated values to outData. Use separation/align/cohesion with the
provided radii and weights, average neighbor contributions, and apply the
boundary force when a component exceeds boundSize*0.4 (push toward center
using boundForce and boundSize*0.5 scaling). Update velocity by accel*dt,
clamp speed to maxSpeed, then update position by vel*dt.

**Critical Requirements:**
- Guard against gl_GlobalInvocationID.x >= N
- Skip self (i == j) and zero-distance neighbors
- Average the accumulated forces by neighbor count (avoid division by zero)
- Clamp velocity magnitude to maxSpeed


**Flocking Parameters (constant across all subpasses):**
Separation radius: 2.0, weight: 1.5
Alignment radius: 5.0, weight: 1.0
Cohesion radius: 8.0, weight: 1.0
Max speed: 5.0
Bound size: 50.0, bound force: 0.5

**Test Configurations:**
Subpass 0: N=128, timesteps=50, dt=0.05
Subpass 1: N=256, timesteps=100, dt=0.05
Subpass 2: N=512, timesteps=100, dt=0.05
Subpass 3: N=1024, timesteps=200, dt=0.05
Subpass 4: N=2048, timesteps=200, dt=0.05
Subpass 5: N=4096, timesteps=200, dt=0.05

Write the complete GLSL compute shader source code.
The shader performs ONE step. It will be dispatched multiple times with
ping-pong buffers for the required number of timesteps.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Write a GPU compute shader in GLSL that simulates flocking behaviour (boids). Each boid follows three simple rules — steer towards neighbours, match their velocity, and avoid collisions — yet the flock as a whole produces complex, lifelike motion.

The shader runs iteratively on the GPU and final boid positions are compared against a CPU reference. Subpasses increase the flock size (up to thousands) and timestep count, testing parallel neighbourhood queries and numerical accuracy.

Typical Prompt:
Write a Python autopilot for a twin-engine airliner (A320-class).

**Function Signature:**
```python
def autopilot_step(state: dict, dt: float) -> dict:
```

**Input:** `state` is a dict with 212 keys representing redundant sensor data:

**Barometric Altimeters (x3)** (9 keys):
baro_alt_1_ft
baro_alt_1_valid
baro_alt_2_ft
baro_alt_2_valid
baro_alt_3_ft
baro_alt_3_valid
baro_setting_1_inhg
baro_setting_2_inhg
baro_setting_3_inhg

**Radar Altimeters (x3)** (6 keys):
radar_alt_1_ft
radar_alt_1_valid
radar_alt_2_ft
radar_alt_2_valid
radar_alt_3_ft
radar_alt_3_valid

**Airspeed / Mach (x3 ADCs)** (12 keys):
ias_1_kt
ias_1_valid
ias_2_kt
ias_2_valid
ias_3_kt
ias_3_valid
mach_1
mach_2
mach_3
tas_1_kt
tas_2_kt
tas_3_kt

**Angle of Attack (x3)** (6 keys):
aoa_1_deg
aoa_1_valid
aoa_2_deg
aoa_2_valid
aoa_3_deg
aoa_3_valid

**GPS (x3)** (18 keys):
gps_1_alt_ft
gps_1_gs_kt
gps_1_lat
gps_1_lon
gps_1_track_deg
gps_1_valid
gps_2_alt_ft
gps_2_gs_kt
gps_2_lat
gps_2_lon
gps_2_track_deg
gps_2_valid
gps_3_alt_ft
gps_3_gs_kt
gps_3_lat
gps_3_lon
gps_3_track_deg
gps_3_valid

**IRS / INS (x3)** (27 keys):
irs_1_gs_kt
irs_1_heading_deg
irs_1_lat
irs_1_lon
irs_1_pitch_deg
irs_1_roll_deg
irs_1_track_deg
irs_1_valid
irs_1_vs_fpm
irs_2_gs_kt
irs_2_heading_deg
irs_2_lat
irs_2_lon
irs_2_pitch_deg
irs_2_roll_deg
irs_2_track_deg
irs_2_valid
irs_2_vs_fpm
irs_3_gs_kt
irs_3_heading_deg
irs_3_lat
irs_3_lon
irs_3_pitch_deg
irs_3_roll_deg
irs_3_track_deg
irs_3_valid
irs_3_vs_fpm

**Gyroscopes (x3)** (12 keys):
gyro_1_pitch_rate_dps
gyro_1_roll_rate_dps
gyro_1_valid
gyro_1_yaw_rate_dps
gyro_2_pitch_rate_dps
gyro_2_roll_rate_dps
gyro_2_valid
gyro_2_yaw_rate_dps
gyro_3_pitch_rate_dps
gyro_3_roll_rate_dps
gyro_3_valid
gyro_3_yaw_rate_dps

**Accelerometers (x3)** (12 keys):
accel_1_valid
accel_1_x_g
accel_1_y_g
accel_1_z_g
accel_2_valid
accel_2_x_g
accel_2_y_g
accel_2_z_g
accel_3_valid
accel_3_x_g
accel_3_y_g
accel_3_z_g

**Air Data Computers (x3)** (15 keys):
adc_1_density_alt_ft
adc_1_oat_c
adc_1_pressure_alt_ft
adc_1_tat_c
adc_1_valid
adc_2_density_alt_ft
adc_2_oat_c
adc_2_pressure_alt_ft
adc_2_tat_c
adc_2_valid
adc_3_density_alt_ft
adc_3_oat_c
adc_3_pressure_alt_ft
adc_3_tat_c
adc_3_valid

**Flight Controls** (11 keys):
aileron_pos_deg
elevator_pos_deg
elevator_trim_deg
flap_pos_left_deg
flap_pos_right_deg
rudder_pos_deg
slat_pos_left_deg
slat_pos_right_deg
speedbrake_handle
spoiler_pos_left
spoiler_pos_right

**Landing Gear** (10 keys):
gear_handle_down
gear_left_locked
gear_left_pos
gear_nose_locked
gear_nose_pos
gear_right_locked
gear_right_pos
weight_on_wheels_left
weight_on_wheels_nose
weight_on_wheels_right

**Engines (x2)** (24 keys):
engine_1_bleed_air_psi
engine_1_egt_c
engine_1_epr
engine_1_ff_kgph
engine_1_fire
engine_1_n1_pct
engine_1_n2_pct
engine_1_oil_press_psi
engine_1_oil_temp_c
engine_1_running
engine_1_thrust_pct
engine_1_vibration
engine_2_bleed_air_psi
engine_2_egt_c
engine_2_epr
engine_2_ff_kgph
engine_2_fire
engine_2_n1_pct
engine_2_n2_pct
engine_2_oil_press_psi
engine_2_oil_temp_c
engine_2_running
engine_2_thrust_pct
engine_2_vibration

**Throttle** (2 keys):
throttle_1_pos
throttle_2_pos

**Fuel** (4 keys):
fuel_tank_center_kg
fuel_tank_left_kg
fuel_tank_right_kg
fuel_total_kg

**Weight/CG** (2 keys):
cg_pct_mac
gross_weight_kg

**Environment** (6 keys):
icing_rate
oat_c
tat_c
turbulence_intensity
wind_dir_deg
wind_speed_kt

**Navigation** (8 keys):
drift_angle_deg
flight_path_angle_deg
ground_speed_kt
magnetic_heading_deg
magnetic_variation_deg
track_deg
true_heading_deg
vertical_speed_fpm

**Electrical** (7 keys):
apu_gen_active
battery_voltage
bus_1_voltage
bus_2_voltage
bus_ess_voltage
gen_1_active
gen_2_active

**Hydraulic** (6 keys):
hyd_a_press_psi
hyd_a_qty_pct
hyd_b_press_psi
hyd_b_qty_pct
hyd_c_press_psi
hyd_c_qty_pct

**Pneumatic/Cabin** (7 keys):
apu_bleed_press_psi
bleed_1_press_psi
bleed_2_press_psi
cabin_alt_ft
cabin_vs_fpm
pack_1_active
pack_2_active

**Autopilot Targets** (6 keys):
ap_engaged
at_engaged
target_alt_ft
target_hdg_deg
target_ias_kt
target_vs_fpm

**Sim** (2 keys):
dt
sim_time_s

Key facts:
- Most sensors come in triples (3 redundant units). Any can fail silently!
- `*_valid` flags exist but may be WRONG (sensor can fail without flagging)
- `target_alt_ft`, `target_hdg_deg`, `target_ias_kt` are the desired flight state
- `dt` is the timestep (~0.05s = 20 Hz)

**Output:** Return a dict with these control keys:
```
elevator: -1.0 (nose down) to +1.0 (nose up), RELATIVE TO TRIM
(0.0 = maintain current trimmed attitude)
aileron: -1.0 (roll left) to +1.0 (roll right)
rudder: -1.0 (yaw left) to +1.0 (yaw right)
throttle_1: 0.0 (idle) to 1.0 (full thrust) — left engine
throttle_2: 0.0 (idle) to 1.0 (full thrust) — right engine
elevator_trim: -1.0 to +1.0 (adjusts the trim baseline over time)
flaps: 0 to 40 (degrees)
speedbrake: 0.0 to 1.0
gear: 0 (up) or 1 (down)
```
NOTE: If you omit a key, the current value is maintained. Throttle defaults
to the current setting if not specified.

**Critical Design Requirements:**
1. **Sensor fusion**: Cross-check redundant sensors, and cross-check other physical
properties. If GPS altitude is dropping, radar altitude is stable, and barometric
altitude is climbing - two failures have occured, and you have to pick wisely.
2. **Aerodynamic simulation**: The flight model includes a full aerodynamic model, so
brush up on your knowledge of stalls, asymetric thurst, yaw dampening, and trim.
5. Vne is (~365 KIAS / Mach 0.82).
6. **Persistence**: You may define module-level variables for PID integral terms,
previous values, etc. They persist across calls within a scenario.

Your single autopilot function must handle every scenario, including over a dozen
historical aviation accidents. The same code is tested against every scenario.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)

Write a Python autopilot for a simulated twin-engine airliner. The autopilot receives over 200 sensor readings each tick (altitude, airspeed, attitude, engine data, etc.) — many triple-redundant — and must output control surface commands (elevator, aileron, rudder, throttle) to keep the aircraft flying safely.

The 30 scenarios range from calm cruise to catastrophic failures inspired by real aviation disasters: frozen pitot tubes, stuck trim, engine flameouts, sensor disagreements, and severe turbulence. The AI must handle sensor fusion, fault detection, and adaptive control.

Test 52: Self-Driving Car Highway Autopilot (Python) Score: 0.00 / 25
Typical Prompt:
Write a Python autopilot for a self-driving car on a multi-lane freeway.
All units are METRIC (m, m/s, km/h, degrees).

**Function Signature:**
```python
def autopilot_step(state: dict, dt: float) -> dict:
```

**Input:** `state` is a dict with 117 keys from redundant sensors:

**Wheel Speed (x4)** (8 keys):
wheel_speed_fl_kmh
wheel_speed_fl_valid
wheel_speed_fr_kmh
wheel_speed_fr_valid
wheel_speed_rl_kmh
wheel_speed_rl_valid
wheel_speed_rr_kmh
wheel_speed_rr_valid

**Gyroscopes (x3)** (6 keys):
gyro_1_valid
gyro_1_yaw_rate_dps
gyro_2_valid
gyro_2_yaw_rate_dps
gyro_3_valid
gyro_3_yaw_rate_dps

**Accelerometers (x3)** (9 keys):
accel_1_lateral_ms2
accel_1_longitudinal_ms2
accel_1_valid
accel_2_lateral_ms2
accel_2_longitudinal_ms2
accel_2_valid
accel_3_lateral_ms2
accel_3_longitudinal_ms2
accel_3_valid

**GPS (x2)** (10 keys):
gps_1_heading_deg
gps_1_speed_kmh
gps_1_valid
gps_1_x_m
gps_1_y_m
gps_2_heading_deg
gps_2_speed_kmh
gps_2_valid
gps_2_x_m
gps_2_y_m

**Steering Angle (x2)** (4 keys):
steering_angle_1_deg
steering_angle_1_valid
steering_angle_2_deg
steering_angle_2_valid

**Brake Pressure (x2)** (4 keys):
brake_pressure_1_pct
brake_pressure_1_valid
brake_pressure_2_pct
brake_pressure_2_valid

**Vehicle Speed (x2)** (2 keys):
vehicle_speed_1_kmh
vehicle_speed_2_kmh

**Lidar Beams (12 angles)** (24 keys):
lidar_+005_deg_range_m
lidar_+005_deg_valid
lidar_+015_deg_range_m
lidar_+015_deg_valid
lidar_+030_deg_range_m
lidar_+030_deg_valid
lidar_+045_deg_range_m
lidar_+045_deg_valid
lidar_+060_deg_range_m
lidar_+060_deg_valid
lidar_+090_deg_range_m
lidar_+090_deg_valid
lidar_-005_deg_range_m
lidar_-005_deg_valid
lidar_-015_deg_range_m
lidar_-015_deg_valid
lidar_-030_deg_range_m
lidar_-030_deg_valid
lidar_-045_deg_range_m
lidar_-045_deg_valid
lidar_-060_deg_range_m
lidar_-060_deg_valid
lidar_-090_deg_range_m
lidar_-090_deg_valid

**Vision Systems (x3)** (42 keys):
vision_1_lane_offset_m
vision_1_left_line_dist_m
vision_1_left_line_type
vision_1_left_line_valid
vision_1_obstacle_detected
vision_1_obstacle_dist_m
vision_1_obstacle_rel_speed_kmh
vision_1_obstacle_valid
vision_1_right_line_dist_m
vision_1_right_line_type
vision_1_right_line_valid
vision_1_road_curvature_1pm
vision_1_speed_limit_kmh
vision_1_speed_limit_valid
vision_2_lane_offset_m
vision_2_left_line_dist_m
vision_2_left_line_type
vision_2_left_line_valid
vision_2_obstacle_detected
vision_2_obstacle_dist_m
vision_2_obstacle_rel_speed_kmh
vision_2_obstacle_valid
vision_2_right_line_dist_m
vision_2_right_line_type
vision_2_right_line_valid
vision_2_road_curvature_1pm
vision_2_speed_limit_kmh
vision_2_speed_limit_valid
vision_3_lane_offset_m
vision_3_left_line_dist_m
vision_3_left_line_type
vision_3_left_line_valid
vision_3_obstacle_detected
vision_3_obstacle_dist_m
vision_3_obstacle_rel_speed_kmh
vision_3_obstacle_valid
vision_3_right_line_dist_m
vision_3_right_line_type
vision_3_right_line_valid
vision_3_road_curvature_1pm
vision_3_speed_limit_kmh
vision_3_speed_limit_valid

**Traction/Surface** (3 keys):
estimated_mu
rain_sensor
temperature_c

**Targets** (3 keys):
current_lane
target_lane
target_speed_kmh

**Sim** (2 keys):
dt
sim_time_s

Key facts:
- Wheel speed sensors (4): FL, FR, RL, RR in km/h. Any can fail.
- Gyroscopes (3): yaw rate in degrees/second. Any can fail silently.
- Accelerometers (3): longitudinal and lateral in m/s². Any can fail.
- GPS (2): position in meters, speed in km/h. Can lose signal.
- Lidar (12 beams): range in meters at angles from -90° to +90°.
- Vision (3 independent systems): lane line distances, types (solid/dashed),
obstacle detection (bool + distance + relative speed), speed limit signs,
lane offset, road curvature. ANY can fail or flicker!
- `estimated_mu`: estimated tire-road friction (1.0=dry, <0.3=ice).
- `target_speed_kmh`: desired speed. `target_lane`: desired lane (0-2).
- `*_valid` flags exist but may be WRONG on failed sensors.
- Sensors can FLICKER: randomly return invalid data for a few frames.

**Output:** Return a dict:
```
steering: -1.0 (full left) to +1.0 (full right)
0.0 = straight ahead
throttle: 0.0 (no power) to 1.0 (full power)
brake: 0.0 (no brake) to 1.0 (emergency stop)
```
If you omit a key, the current value is maintained.

**Critical Design Requirements:**
1. **Sensor fusion**: Cross-check redundant sensors. Median/voting for disagreements.
2. **Lane keeping**: Stay centered in lane using vision line distances and lidar.
3. **Speed control**: Maintain target speed, respect speed limits.
4. **Obstacle avoidance**: If obstacle detected, brake and/or lane change.
5. **Traction management**: On low-mu surfaces, reduce speed, gentler inputs.
6. **Graceful degradation**: Handle sensor failures, flickering data.
7. **Persistence**: Module-level variables persist across calls for filtering/PID.

**Test Scenarios (25 total):**
0. Normal highway cruise
1. Gentle curve
2. Slow vehicle ahead
3. Emergency stop — stopped vehicle
4. Cut-in from adjacent lane
5. Ice patch — reduced traction
6. Aquaplaning — very low friction
7. Wheel speed sensor failure (FL)
8. Two wheel speed sensors fail
9. Gyro sensor failure
10. GPS signal lost
11. Vision system 1 failure
12. All vision systems flickering
13. Front lidar beams fail
14. Steering angle sensors disagree
15. Total brake failure
16. Throttle stuck at 30%
17. Road debris
18. Dense traffic
19. Crosswind gust
20. Steering partially jammed
21. Ice + stopped vehicle
22. Flickering sensors in curve
23. Reduced visibility
24. Multi-failure + obstacle

Your single autopilot function must handle ALL scenarios.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought

Write a Python autopilot for a simulated car on a multi-lane freeway. The car receives around 150 sensor readings each tick — redundant wheel speeds, gyros, accelerometers, laser range finders, and camera-based object detection — and must output steering and throttle/brake commands to drive safely.

The 25 scenarios include normal highway driving, sudden obstacles, sensor failures, loss of traction (ice and aquaplaning), tire blowouts, and flickering sensors. The AI must fuse noisy data, detect faults, and react in real time.

Test 53: Spacecraft Orbital Docking Autopilot (Python) Score: 0.00 / 25
Typical Prompt:
Write a Python autopilot to dock a spacecraft with a space station in orbit.

**CRITICAL ORBITAL MECHANICS INSIGHT:**
In orbit, relative motion is COUNTER-INTUITIVE:
- Thrusting TOWARD the target (prograde if behind) raises your orbit → you SLOW DOWN relative to target
- To catch up: thrust RETROGRADE (backward!) to lower orbit → you go FASTER
- Radial thrusts cause oscillation, not direct movement
- Cross-track corrections require thrusts at specific orbital positions
- Near the target (<50m), approximate as linear (CW equations work)
- Far away (>200m), full orbital mechanics dominate

**Function Signature:**
```python
def autopilot_step(state: dict, dt: float) -> dict:
```

**Input:** `state` is a dict with 110 keys:

**Range Finders (x3)** (6 keys):
range_1_m
range_1_valid
range_2_m
range_2_valid
range_3_m
range_3_valid

**Radar Relative Pos/Vel** (12 keys):
radar_rel_pos_x_m
radar_rel_pos_y_m
radar_rel_pos_z_m
radar_rel_vel_x_ms
radar_rel_vel_y_ms
radar_rel_vel_z_ms
radar_rel_x_valid
radar_rel_y_valid
radar_rel_z_valid
radar_relv_x_valid
radar_relv_y_valid
radar_relv_z_valid

**Lidar Relative Pos/Vel** (12 keys):
lidar_rel_pos_x_m
lidar_rel_pos_y_m
lidar_rel_pos_z_m
lidar_rel_vel_x_ms
lidar_rel_vel_y_ms
lidar_rel_vel_z_ms
lidar_rel_x_valid
lidar_rel_y_valid
lidar_rel_z_valid
lidar_relv_x_valid
lidar_relv_y_valid
lidar_relv_z_valid

**Gyroscopes (x3)** (18 keys):
gyro_1_pitch_dps
gyro_1_pitch_valid
gyro_1_roll_dps
gyro_1_roll_valid
gyro_1_yaw_dps
gyro_1_yaw_valid
gyro_2_pitch_dps
gyro_2_pitch_valid
gyro_2_roll_dps
gyro_2_roll_valid
gyro_2_yaw_dps
gyro_2_yaw_valid
gyro_3_pitch_dps
gyro_3_pitch_valid
gyro_3_roll_dps
gyro_3_roll_valid
gyro_3_yaw_dps
gyro_3_yaw_valid

**Accelerometers (x3)** (18 keys):
accel_1_x_ms2
accel_1_x_valid
accel_1_y_ms2
accel_1_y_valid
accel_1_z_ms2
accel_1_z_valid
accel_2_x_ms2
accel_2_x_valid
accel_2_y_ms2
accel_2_y_valid
accel_2_z_ms2
accel_2_z_valid
accel_3_x_ms2
accel_3_x_valid
accel_3_y_ms2
accel_3_y_valid
accel_3_z_ms2
accel_3_z_valid

**Star Trackers (x2)** (12 keys):
star_tracker_1_pitch_deg
star_tracker_1_pitch_valid
star_tracker_1_roll_deg
star_tracker_1_roll_valid
star_tracker_1_yaw_deg
star_tracker_1_yaw_valid
star_tracker_2_pitch_deg
star_tracker_2_pitch_valid
star_tracker_2_roll_deg
star_tracker_2_roll_valid
star_tracker_2_yaw_deg
star_tracker_2_yaw_valid

**GPS (x2)** (12 keys):
gps_1_pos_x_m
gps_1_pos_y_m
gps_1_pos_z_m
gps_1_x_valid
gps_1_y_valid
gps_1_z_valid
gps_2_pos_x_m
gps_2_pos_y_m
gps_2_pos_z_m
gps_2_x_valid
gps_2_y_valid
gps_2_z_valid

**Ground Station Data** (6 keys):
ground_altitude_km
ground_altitude_km_valid
ground_semi_major_axis_m
ground_semi_major_axis_m_valid
ground_speed_ms
ground_speed_ms_valid

**Docking Camera** (8 keys):
dock_camera_active
dock_camera_bearing_x
dock_camera_bearing_x_valid
dock_camera_bearing_y
dock_camera_bearing_y_valid
dock_camera_bearing_z
dock_camera_bearing_z_valid
dock_camera_range_m

**Fuel** (2 keys):
fuel_remaining_kg
fuel_remaining_pct

**Targets** (2 keys):
target_dock_range_m
target_dock_speed_ms

Key sensor notes:
- Relative position/velocity in LVLH frame: x=radial (up), y=along-track (velocity dir), z=cross-track
- NEGATIVE y = behind station (most common start). Must CLOSE the y gap.
- Range finders give scalar distance. Radar/lidar give 3D relative pos/vel.
- All sensors can fail silently! Cross-check redundant sensors.
- Docking camera only active within 500m. Gives bearing to docking port.
- Ground station data updates every ~30s (stale but reliable).

**Output:** Return a dict:
```
thrust_x: -1.0 to +1.0 (radial: + = away from Earth)
thrust_y: -1.0 to +1.0 (along-track: + = prograde)
thrust_z: -1.0 to +1.0 (cross-track: + = north)
att_rate_roll: rad/s (max ±0.035)
att_rate_pitch: rad/s (max ±0.035)
att_rate_yaw: rad/s (max ±0.035)
```
Each thrust axis: 220N per thruster, Isp=290s. Fuel is LIMITED (~500 kg).

**Docking Success Criteria:**
- Range < 2m AND relative speed < 0.3 m/s

**Approach Strategy Hints:**
1. Far (>200m): Use Hohmann-like transfer — retrograde to lower orbit if behind
2. Medium (50-200m): Gentle corrections, V-bar approach preferred
3. Close (<50m): Linear regime, direct thrust corrections OK
4. Final (<10m): Very gentle, < 0.1 m/s approach speed

**Test Scenarios (25 total):**
0. V-bar approach — 200m behind
1. V-bar approach — 500m behind
2. V-bar close approach — 50m
3. R-bar approach — 200m below
4. Approach from above — 200m
5. Cross-track offset — 100m
6. Ahead of station — 200m
7. Diagonal — 150m radial + 150m along-track
8. Long-range — 2km behind
9. Drifting away — 200m with 0.5 m/s radial
10. Closing too fast — 2 m/s approach
11. Range sensor 1 failed
12. Radar relative position failed
13. Gyro 1 bias + gyro 2 drift
14. Star tracker 1 dead
15. Both GPS receivers failed
16. Docking camera failed
17. Uncommanded +Y thrust (0.5N)
18. Uncommanded cross-track thrust
19. Flickering range + radar sensors
20. Low fuel — only 50 kg remaining
21. Cross-track + radar failure
22. Higher orbit — 450km
23. All range sensors very noisy
24. Multi-failure + uncommanded thrust + offset

Your single autopilot function must handle ALL scenarios.
Persistence: module-level variables persist across calls within a scenario.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt

View chain of thought

Write a Python autopilot to dock a spacecraft with a space station in orbit. Orbital mechanics are counter-intuitive — thrusting towards the target often moves you away because of how orbits work — so the AI must understand rendezvous dynamics, not just “point and go”.

The 25 scenarios vary starting positions, sensor and thruster failures, and uncommanded thrust events. The AI must plan approach manoeuvres, manage fuel, and handle faults while achieving a gentle, aligned docking.
